summary: I am from Nagasaki, Japan, where I was born in 1885 during the Meiji era. My elder sister lived in the city of Nagasaki and helped arrange my marriage. It was a place where my family had roots, and I left there to come to America in 1915.

I was born in Meiji 18, which is 1885. The Meiji period was a time of change in Japan, and I had some education during that era. My father, a calligrapher, was strict and taught me many things, reflecting the values of that time.

My father served under the Saga area feudal lord, and so did my maternal grandfather. Families like ours who served the lordship were considered higher society in our village. This background influenced how we lived and how strict my parents were with us.

I probably learned about Sontoku Ninomiya, the scholar, when I was young, but my memory isn't very clear now. He was part of the education and moral teachings that influenced me growing up in Japan. His lessons were likely part of what my father taught me.

My parents were Zenshū Buddhists, and my father had a daily ritual of praying in all directions and worshipping the God of Water. Religion was important in our household, though I did not personally believe in any religion until much later in life. My father's devotion was a strong influence on me.

I became a member of the Seventh-day Adventist Church when I was 47 after my husband passed away. A Seventh-day Adventist minister, Rev. Nagaki, taught me the Bible at my home, which helped me stay healthy and strong to raise my five children. I have been a member for almost 45 years now.

My husband was from Iyo Province on Shikoku Island in Japan. He came to America to study and later worked for the U.S. Government in Seattle. His background from Iyo was part of his identity, though I was from Nagasaki.

I came to Seattle in 1915 when I was about 30 years old. My husband was working there for the U.S. Government, and most of my children were born in Seattle. I thought Seattle was a nice place and lived there until 1924 before moving to California.
After Seattle, we moved to Mount View, California, where my husband passed away in 1950 at age 56. We lived there from 1924 until 1938. It was a significant place for our family during those years.
In 1938, we moved from Mount View to Oakland, California. This was where we lived before the war broke out. Oakland became part of our life as we raised our children and faced the challenges ahead.

I don't remember well where I was when Pearl Harbor was attacked in 1941. My daughter said that my son and I moved to Sacramento when the war broke out. It was a difficult time for all Japanese Americans, including my family.

My son and I were evacuated to the Walerga Assembly Center during World War II. It was the first place we were sent before being moved to other relocation centers. This was part of the difficult experience of the war years.

After Walerga, we were sent to Tule Lake War Relocation Center. My son went into the U.S. Army from there before Tule Lake became a segregated center. I stayed out of the troubles there and did not get involved in the conflicts at Tule Lake.

After Tule Lake became segregated, I was moved to Topaz War Relocation Center. I do not remember exactly how long I stayed there, but it was part of the wartime experience. I was active in visiting people and doing evangelistic work while in camp.

My daughter was married and sent to Minidoka War Relocation Center during the war. I was not with her there, but it was part of our family's experience of relocation. The camps separated families in many cases.

I remember the Japanese-Chinese War when I was young, which included the Japanese invasion of Manchuria. It was a time of conflict in my youth, though I was still quite young then. These events were part of the history I lived through in Japan.
I recall hearing about the Russo-Japanese War, but I was very young at the time. It was one of the significant wars during my early years in Japan. These conflicts shaped the era I grew up in.

My husband worked for the United States government after coming to America. He studied here and then got a government job in Seattle. This employment allowed our family to live in the U.S. and eventually brought me here in 1915.

When the war broke out, my son and I moved to Sacramento. It was during the time of evacuation and relocation of Japanese Americans. Sacramento was a temporary place before we were sent to the assembly centers.

Los Angeles was mentioned but I did not live there myself. It was part of the broader Japanese American community during the war. My family was mostly in Seattle, Mount View, Oakland, and the relocation centers.
